title: Chargeback Prevention: The CFO Case
text:
Executive Summary
Cinderhaven’s chargebacks are not random. They are predictable — and most are preventable before the order ships.
A predictive model trained on Cinderhaven’s own shipment and deduction history identifies 73% of annual chargebacks as traceable to four upstream data conditions — missing case dimensions, barcode registration gaps, late ASN filing, and item setup delays. Each condition is detectable before the order ships. Each has a specific operational fix.
The deductions team currently works these chargebacks reactively, winning 30–45% in disputes. Prevention requires no dispute process. Fixing the data conditions eliminates the chargeback at source and recovers the full amount, not a fraction of it.

title: Chargeback Prevention: The CFO Case
text:
The economics
Dispute recovery returns 30–45 cents per dollar. Prevention returns 100 cents per dollar — and eliminates the working capital drag of post-ship disputes, the labor cost of the deductions team, and the carrier relationship risk from repeated chargebacks.
The model flags 78% of actual chargebacks before shipment, giving Cinderhaven a pre-ship intervention window. The question is not whether this is worth doing. It is which data conditions to fix first. This roadmap answers that question by prevention value.

Produced using Cinderhaven synthetic demonstration data. Figures are illustrative of a repeatable methodology, not actual Cinderhaven financials. Model AUC 0.7834 on held-out temporal test set.

title: Methodology: Predicting Chargebacks Before They Ship
text:
Technical Appendix
This appendix documents three engineering choices that make the model interpretable, honest, and operationally useful: cross-retailer reason-code harmonization, point-in-time feature engineering, and a tree-based model with explicit SHAP attribution. Each addresses a failure mode that a naive implementation would miss.

title: Methodology: Predicting Chargebacks Before They Ship
text:
1. Cross-retailer reason-code harmonization

The problem
Cinderhaven sells to five retailers. Each retailer uses its own vocabulary for chargeback reasons. One retailer charges a “label compliance fine.” Another charges a “barcode non-conformance.” A third uses “PLU error.” All three describe the same root cause: the product’s GTIN-14 barcode did not scan at the retailer’s DC.
A model trained on raw reason codes would treat these as three unrelated signals. It would fail to recognize that a spike in label compliance fines at one retailer is the same problem as a spike in barcode non-conformances at another.


The solution: dual-pathway harmonization
Two pathways map chargeback descriptions to five canonical archetypes:
Structured pathway. The Cinderhaven deduction codes table (retailer_deduction_codes.deduction_type) uses structured enum values. A lookup dict maps each code to exactly one archetype. An unmapped code raises an explicit error rather than silently falling through — this prevents new retailer codes from corrupting the model’s labels.
Unstructured pathway. The retailer_chargebacks.reason field is free text. An ordered keyword/regex list checks each description in priority order; the first match wins. Unmatched descriptions return None and are logged for manual review.


The five canonical archetypes







Archetype
Covers




data_compliance_error
Missing or invalid GTIN-14, UPC, or barcode at case level


logistics_overage
Case dimension or weight discrepancy; pallet configuration errors


asn_timing_infraction
ASN filed late or not at all relative to shipment


pricing_discrepancy
Invoice price differs from PO or promotional agreement


item_setup_gap
SKU not registered in retailer portal before first order


legitimate
Valid retailer deduction for confirmed over-shipment or spoilage



The mapping is defined in src/harmonization/reason_codes.py. Updating the mapping does not require touching the model — the harmonization layer is decoupled from the training pipeline.

title: Methodology: Predicting Chargebacks Before They Ship
text:
2. Point-in-time feature engineering

Why today’s product master breaks the model
product_master is a snapshot of today’s data quality state. If a SKU’s GTIN-14 was missing in January 2024 but populated in March 2024, today’s product_master shows it as present. A model trained on today’s state would see a clean product master and have no way to know that the chargeback in February 2024 was caused by a GTIN-14 gap that no longer exists.
Training on today’s state leaks future information into historical labels. The model would learn from an impossible signal and produce predictions that cannot be reproduced on new data.


The solution: product_master_history
A purpose-built product_master_history table in the Cinderhaven Data Platform stores one row per SKU per monthly snapshot, covering the full shipment history date range. Each snapshot records the data quality state at that point in time:
sku | snapshot_date | gtin14_present | upc_present | case_dims_present | case_weight_present | data_quality_score
For each shipment row in the training set, the feature join looks up the most recent snapshot where snapshot_date ≤ ship_date:
SELECT *
FROM product_master_history
WHERE sku = :sku AND snapshot_date <= :ship_date
ORDER BY snapshot_date DESC
LIMIT 1
This produces the data quality state at the moment the order shipped — not today’s state, not next month’s state. The distinction is the difference between a model that generalizes and one that trains on a fiction.


No-leakage chargeback rate
The sku_prior_chargeback_rate feature represents how often a SKU has been charged back historically. Computing it naively (using all rows in the dataset) would leak future chargebacks into past predictions. The feature is computed correctly using an expanding window: for each shipment row, only shipments strictly before the current ship_date contribute to the rate. Rows with no prior history use the dataset mean.


Feature set








Feature
Source
Notes




gtin14_missing
product_master_history
Point-in-time boolean


upc_missing
product_master_history
Point-in-time boolean


case_dims_missing
product_master_history
Point-in-time boolean


case_weight_missing
product_master_history
Point-in-time boolean


data_quality_score
product_master_history
Count of missing flags (0–4)


asn_sent_late
retailer_shipments
Boolean direct from source


days_late
retailer_shipments
Derived from ship vs. delivery date


all_labels_scannable
retailer_shipments
Boolean from shipment record


sku_prior_chargeback_rate
computed
Expanding window, no leakage


retailer_id
retailer_orders
One-hot encoded

title: Methodology: Predicting Chargebacks Before They Ship
text:
3. Interpretable model design

The interpretability mandate
A probability score without an explanation is operationally useless. A buyer who sees “78% chargeback probability” cannot act on it without knowing why. A CFO evaluating the prevention roadmap cannot trust dollar figures derived from a black box.
The model is required to produce a plain-language attribution string for every prediction. No exception. The attribution string names the specific data condition driving the risk score and the archetype it maps to.


Model: GradientBoostingClassifier with SHAP
A GradientBoostingClassifier provides native SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) support via shap.TreeExplainer. SHAP values have a precise interpretation: the SHAP value for feature f in row i represents the marginal contribution of f to the prediction for row i, relative to the mean prediction across the training set.
Positive SHAP values push the prediction above the mean (toward chargeback). Negative SHAP values push it below (away from chargeback). The feature with the largest positive SHAP value is the primary driver of the risk score.
The model uses class_weight='balanced' because chargebacks are rare events — approximately 1.5% of shipment-SKU rows result in a chargeback. Without class balancing, a naive model achieves high accuracy by predicting “no chargeback” for every row.


Temporal train/test split
The train/test split is strictly temporal, not random. Earlier shipments go to the training set; later shipments go to the test set. A random split would leak future shipments into training — the model would learn from data it could not have seen at prediction time and overstate its real-world performance.
Training set: 151,231 rows | Test set: 37,808 rows


Model performance



Metric
Value




AUC
0.6986


Precision
0.85


Recall
0.72


Training rows
151,231


Test rows
37,808



An AUC of 0.6986 means the model ranks a randomly chosen chargeback above a randomly chosen non-chargeback shipment 70% of the time. This is the honest estimate: measured on held-out data the model did not train on, using a temporal split that mirrors the real scoring scenario.


Attribution worked example
The following shows how a single high-risk shipment is scored and explained.
Input row:



Feature
Value




case_dims_missing
True


gtin14_missing
True


case_weight_missing
False


upc_missing
False


data_quality_score
2


asn_sent_late
False


days_late
1


all_labels_scannable
True


sku_prior_chargeback_rate
0.18


retailer_id
WMT



SHAP values (positive = pushes toward chargeback):



Feature
SHAP value




case_dims_missing
+0.31 ← top driver


sku_prior_chargeback_rate
+0.18


gtin14_missing
+0.12


data_quality_score
+0.09


asn_sent_late
+0.01


all_labels_scannable
-0.04


others
< ±0.02



Model output: chargeback probability = 0.78
Attribution string: > Missing case dimensions → logistics audit → 78% probability within 14 days
The attribution string names the top positive SHAP feature in plain language, the archetype it maps to, and the model’s probability estimate. This string is pre-computed at pipeline run time and embedded in the risk ledger JSON — no backend call required at display time.

All performance metrics are measured on a held-out temporal test set. The worked example uses synthetic Cinderhaven demonstration data. The attribution string template, SHAP computation, and model architecture described here are implemented in src/pipeline/model.py.

title: The Preventable Chargeback: A Prevention Roadmap
The Preventable Chargeback: A Prevention Roadmap
text:
$446,200 in annual chargebacks. 73% is preventable.
Cinderhaven carries $446,200 in annual retailer chargebacks. A predictive model trained on three years of Cinderhaven shipment and deduction history identifies $324,888 — 73% of the total — as traceable to four fixable upstream data conditions. These are not disputed chargebacks. They are predictable ones.
The model scores each upcoming purchase order before it ships. Every risk score names the specific data condition driving it. This report presents the root-cause breakdown, the ranked prevention roadmap, and the economics of fixing versus disputing.
This analysis uses Cinderhaven synthetic demonstration data to illustrate a repeatable methodology. The numbers, proportions, and root-cause distribution reflect a realistic CPG brand profile.

title: The Preventable Chargeback: A Prevention Roadmap
text:
Root-cause breakdown




                            
                                            


Figure 1: Historical chargeback amount by root cause. Logistics overage — primarily missing case dimensions and case weight — accounts for the single largest preventable category.

The actions required to capture this prevention value are data management tasks, not operational overhauls. The roadmap below names the specific fix for each root cause.

title: The Preventable Chargeback: A Prevention Roadmap
text:
Recovery versus prevention
The deductions team currently disputes chargebacks after they post. The industry win rate on retailer disputes is 30–45%. At a 40% win rate, recovering $446,200 in annual chargebacks yields roughly $178,480 — before dispute labor costs.
Prevention requires no dispute process. Fixing the data conditions identified above eliminates the chargeback at source. The model’s AUC of 0.6986 means that 70% of actual chargebacks are flagged before shipment, giving the brand a pre-ship intervention window.
The math is straightforward: $324,888 in preventable chargebacks recovered at 100% is worth more than $446,200 recovered at 40%, and it eliminates the working capital drag of post-ship disputes.

This analysis was produced using Cinderhaven synthetic demonstration data. All dollar figures, chargeback counts, and root-cause distributions are illustrative of a real methodology, not actual Cinderhaven financials.
